title: GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
GDP per capita, 2020
This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The choropleth map displays global GDP per capita for the year 2020, with values expressed in international dollars at 1990 prices to account for inflation and cost-of-living differences. A continuous color scale ranges from light green for the lowest income bracket of $0 to $1,000, transitioning through teal and medium blue, up to dark blue representing the highest bracket of $20,000 and above. The highest values are concentrated in North America, Western Europe, and Oceania. The United States, Canada, Australia, and Norway appear in the darkest blue, placing them in the top tier above $20,000. Western European nations including the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and Sweden also fall within this highest bracket. In contrast, the lowest values are visible across much of sub-Saharan Africa. Countries such as Niger, Chad, the Central African Republic, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo are shaded in the lightest green, indicating GDP per capita between $0 and $1,000. Large parts of South Asia and Southeast Asia, including India and Indonesia, occupy the lower-middle range between $1,000 and $5,000. South American nations like Brazil and Argentina sit in the middle bands, generally between $5,000 and $10,000. Russia and China appear in the medium blue shades, corresponding to the $5,000 to $10,000 range. Greenland and several African nations are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no available data. The distribution shows a clear geographic clustering of high-income economies in the Global North and low-income economies in the Global South.
